questions.prompt: No searching: how does a Docker image differ from a container?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Multiple containers can be created from one image.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Multiple containers can be created from one image.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Multiple containers can be created from one image.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: An image is an immutable template used to create containers.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An image is an immutable template used to create containers.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An image is an immutable template used to create containers.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: A container is a runtime instance created from an image.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A container is a runtime instance created from an image.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A container is a runtime instance created from an image.

questions.prompt: Forum check: why use a Docker volume?

answers.text: From what I've seen, to persist data outside a container's writable layer.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. To persist data outside a container's writable layer.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. To persist data outside a container's writable layer.

answers.text: The practical answer is: to bake source code permanently into an image.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data.

answers.text: My answer: A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.

questions.prompt: This keeps starting arguments: does exposing a port in a Dockerfile publish it to the host?

answers.text: The practical answer is: `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.

answers.text: My answer: A port must appear in EXPOSE before `docker run -p` can publish it.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE.

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Yes, EXPOSE automatically creates a host mapping.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation.

questions.prompt: Forum check: why copy dependency manifests before application source in many Dockerfiles?

answers.text: From what I've seen, a change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.

answers.text: The practical answer is: the dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.

answers.text: My answer: The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: how does a bind mount differ from a named volume?

answers.text: Short version: A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.

answers.text: I'd go with this: A bind mount is stored only inside the image.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.

questions.prompt: I've seen mixed takes, so: why use a multi-stage Docker build?

answers.text: I think the important part is this: every stage runs as a separate production container.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, a multi-stage build automatically updates every base image to its newest version.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.

questions.prompt: Quick gut check: why does PID 1 behavior matter inside a container?

answers.text: I'd go with this: An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: the main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, pID 1 cannot receive termination signals.

feedback.legit: Not this one. PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly.
feedback.sus: Good catch. PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: is an image environment variable a safe place for a permanent secret?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Yes, image configuration is invisible to anyone who pulls it.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values.

answers.text: From what I've seen, secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.

answers.text: The practical answer is: removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: what does a Docker health check indicate?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, that every downstream dependency is permanently available.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A health command only tests what it was designed to observe.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A health command only tests what it was designed to observe.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: A Docker health check by itself always restarts an unhealthy container.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: does basic Compose `depends_on` prove a dependency is ready to serve requests?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: Yes, process start order guarantees application readiness.

feedback.legit: Not this one. A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic.
feedback.sus: Good catch. A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.

questions.prompt: Tiny knowledge check: how do containers discover each other on a user-defined Docker network?

answers.text: Unless I'm mixing things up, they can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.

answers.text: This is what trips people up: They must all publish ports to the public host interface.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: why run a container process as a non-root user?

answers.text: The way I understand it: it makes host kernel vulnerabilities impossible.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks.

answers.text: Short version: A non-root process cannot listen on any network port.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed.

answers.text: I'd go with this: A Dockerfile USER instruction automatically changes ownership of files copied earlier.

feedback.legit: Not this one. USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown.
feedback.sus: Good catch. USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown.
